source: half_baked_feature_detector (2026-05-30) — surfaced after open queue cleared
title: 7 tool modules ship with zero test coverage
component: tools/tools_calendar.py, tools_gmail.py, tools_briefing.py, tools_image.py, tools_video_gen.py, tools_browser_auto.py, tools_computer_use.py
what_failed: half-baked detector flags 7 tool files with no corresponding testing/test_*.py. These are user-facing capabilities (calendar, gmail, image/video gen, browser + computer automation, daily briefing) with no regression protection — a refactor or dependency bump could silently break them.
why_likely: These are external-API wrappers added in feature sprints; testing them needs dependency mocking, so they were shipped without tests. The pure-logic parts (formatting, config detection, graceful-degrade error paths) are testable without network.
migration_plan: 1. Test the no-network surface of each module: pure formatters, is_configured(), and the graceful {success:False,error} paths when the external client is unavailable/raises.
2. Mock the external client (Google service, Playwright, pyautogui, Replicate) — never hit real APIs (mark costly if unavoidable).
3. tools_calendar first (most pure logic). Then briefing, image, video_gen, gmail, browser_auto, computer_use.
4. verify.py PASS with new tests green.
implementation_notes: Adding tests changes no behavior — lowest-risk way to harden the most-exposed tools. Progress tracked per-module in this ticket.
analysis: PERSPECTIVE: The coherence/memory core is now well-tested, but the tool periphery isn't. These wrappers are exactly where a silent break would erode trust (a calendar event silently not created). Lock the graceful-degrade contracts down first — those are the behaviors users actually depend on when creds/network are missing.
progress: 2026-05-30: tools_calendar DONE (testing/test_tools_calendar.py, 13 tests — formatter, is_configured, 5 graceful-degrade paths, summary assembly; no network). REMAINING: briefing, image, video_gen, gmail, browser_auto, computer_use (6).
